lem:arzela-ascoli-c1-compactness.proof_tex: Source: the Arzela--Ascoli theorem, for example Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Theorem 7.25, states that a uniformly bounded equicontinuous family in C(I) on a compact interval I is relatively compact in the sup norm. Applying that theorem separately to a family {f_n} and to its derivative family {f_n'} shows: if sup_n ||f_n||_infty < infinity, sup_n ||f_n'||_infty < infinity, and {f_n'} is equicontinuous, then every sequence has a subsequence converging uniformly together with its derivative. If the class is closed under this joint uniform convergence, it is compact in the C^1(I) topology. This is exactly the classical compactness fact used below.

snapshot.stmt: Let I be a compact interval. If a family F \subset C^1(I) is uniformly bounded in sup norm, its derivative family F' is uniformly bounded and equicontinuous, and F is closed under joint uniform convergence of functions and derivatives, then F is compact in the C^1(I) topology.

node.statement: Let I be a compact interval. If a family F \subset C^1(I) is uniformly bounded in sup norm, its derivative family F' is uniformly bounded and equicontinuous, and F is closed under joint uniform convergence of functions and derivatives, then F is compact in the C^1(I) topology.

lem:endpoint-shape-in-budget.proof_tex: Fix P and a compact budget interval J=[0,\bar M] such that H_*(P,M) is nonempty for every M in J. Write H(M):=H_*(P,M), L(M):=L_*(P,M), and U(M):=U_*(P,M). Because every \mu in H(M) satisfies \mu(c) in [-2,1] pointwise by def:local-extension-class, one has -2 <= L(M) <= U(M) <= 1 for every M, so both endpoint maps are finite. If 0 <= M_1 <= M_2, then H(M_1) \subseteq H(M_2): the interpolation equalities and range restriction are identical, and ess sup |\mu''| <= M_1 implies ess sup |\mu''| <= M_2. Therefore L(M_2) <= L(M_1) and U(M_2) >= U(M_1), so L is weakly decreasing and U is weakly increasing. Now fix M_1,M_2 in J and \lambda in [0,1], and put M_\lambda := (1-\lambda)M_1 + \lambda M_2. If \mu_i in H(M_i) for i=1,2, then \mu_\lambda := (1-\lambda)\mu_1 + \lambda \mu_2 satisfies the same interpolation equalities, still takes values in [-2,1] because that interval is convex, and obeys ess sup |\mu_\lambda''| <= (1-\lambda)M_1 + \lambda M_2 = M_\lambda. Hence \mu_\lambda in H(M_\lambda). Taking \varepsilon-optimal \mu_1,\mu_2 for the upper endpoint and evaluating at \tau_*(P) gives U(M_\lambda) >= (1-\lambda)(U(M_1)-\varepsilon) + \lambda(U(M_2)-\varepsilon); letting \varepsilon downarrow 0 yields U(M_\lambda) >= (1-\lambda)U(M_1)+\lambda U(M_2), so U is concave. The same argument with \varepsilon-optimal lower-endpoint candidates gives L(M_\lambda) <= (1-\lambda)L(M_1)+\lambda L(M_2), so L is convex. Finite convex and concave functions are continuous on the interior of a compact interval, and the proved monotonicity gives one-sided continuity at the endpoints of J. Therefore L is continuous, convex, and weakly decreasing on J, while U is continuous, concave, and weakly increasing on J.

snapshot.stmt: Fix P and \bar M >= 0. Suppose H_*(P,M) is nonempty for every M in [0,\bar M]. Then M \mapsto L_*(P,M) is finite, continuous, convex, and weakly decreasing on [0,\bar M], while M \mapsto U_*(P,M) is finite, continuous, concave, and weakly increasing on [0,\bar M].

defs.def:local-extension-class: H_*(P,M) = {mu in W^{2,\infty}([tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]) : mu(s) = m_P(s) for each s in A_P intersect [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0], ess sup_{c in [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]} |mu''(c)| <= M, and mu(c) in [-2,1] for every c in [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]}
defs.def:selected-endpoints: L_*(P,M) = inf_{mu in H_*(P,M)} mu(tau_*(P)) and U_*(P,M) = sup_{mu in H_*(P,M)} mu(tau_*(P))

lem:generated-score-expansion.proof_tex: Source. Mammen, Rothe, and Schienle (2012, Annals of Statistics, Theorem 1) prove that a second-stage nonparametric estimator with a generated regressor admits an oracle-plus-linear-perturbation expansion, uniformly over the evaluation region, under smoothness of the second-stage map and stochastic equicontinuity of the score process. Hahn and Ridder (2013, Econometrica, Theorem 5) give the corresponding influence-function correction for generated regressors. In the present cross-fitted and Neyman-orthogonal construction, Chernozhukov et al. (2018) imply that the own-observation first-stage term is removed, so under the stated rate conditions \(r_n=o(h_n)\) and \(\sqrt{n h_n}\,r_n^2\to 0\) the remaining generated-score contribution is second order at the local-linear scale. This published package is exactly the classical fact used below.

snapshot.stmt: Consider the cross-fitted orthogonalized local-linear selected-score estimator obtained by replacing the population score \(C\) with the generated score \(\hat C\). Under second-stage smoothness, stochastic equicontinuity of the localized score process, and first-stage rate conditions \(r_n=o(h_n)\) and \(\sqrt{n h_n}\,r_n^2\to 0\), the feasible estimator admits an oracle-plus-linear-perturbation expansion, and after orthogonalization the remaining generated-score effect is second order at the local-linear scale.

lem:oracle-snooping-coverage.proof_tex: Source. Armstrong and Kolesar (2018, Review of Economic Studies, Theorem 3.1 and Corollary 3.1) establish simultaneous honest coverage for a continuum-indexed studentized local-polynomial process via a strong Gaussian approximation and a supremum critical value, under i.i.d. sampling, standard local-polynomial kernel regularity, local density bounded away from zero on the index neighborhood, continuous conditional variance, and shrinking bandwidth with diverging local effective sample size. In the present oracle problem, the deterministic-cutoff process \(\tau \mapsto \tilde V_n(\tau)\) is the same local-linear intercept functional after re-indexing the running variable by \(c-\tau\). Because \(\rho_n \downarrow 0\), \(h_n \to 0\), and `ass:continuous-local-score-density` gives continuity of \(f_{C,P}\) with \(f_{C,P}(\tau_*(P))>0\), there is eventually a neighborhood \(|\tau-\tau_*(P)|\le \rho_n h_n\) on which the score density is bounded away from zero uniformly in the sense needed for the oracle RD approximation. The kernel regularity is built into `def:selected-ll-estimator`, and `ass:local-variance-continuity`, `ass:bandwidth-shrinking`, and `ass:local-effective-sample` supply the remaining classical premises. Therefore the Armstrong-Kolesar strong approximation and supremum critical-value construction apply to the oracle selected-neighborhood process. The standard local-linear orders \(\hat\sigma_n=O_P((n h_n)^{-1/2})\) and \(\hat B_n(M)=O(M h_n^2)\) are the classical RD orders of Calonico, Cattaneo, and Titiunik (2014) and Calonico, Cattaneo, and Farrell (2018).

snapshot.stmt:
Assume `ass:iid-audit`, `ass:continuous-local-score-density`, `ass:continuous-local-regression`, `ass:local-variance-continuity`, `ass:curvature-budget`, `ass:bandwidth-shrinking`, the kernel regularity built into `def:selected-ll-estimator`, and `ass:local-effective-sample`. Let \(\tilde V_n(\tau)\) be the oracle local-linear intercept obtained from `def:selected-ll-estimator` by replacing \(\hat C_i\) with \(C_i\) and evaluating at deterministic cutoff \(\tau\), and let \(\rho_n\downarrow 0\). Then there exists a finite critical value \(cv_{1-\alpha}^{sel}\) such that
\[
\liminf_{n\to\infty}\inf_P P\Bigl(\sup_{|\tau-\tau_*(P)|\le \rho_n h_n}|\tilde V_n(\tau)-g_P(\tau)|\le cv_{1-\alpha}^{sel}\hat\sigma_n+\hat B_n(M)\Bigr)\ge 1-\alpha,
\]
uniformly over \(P\in P_{\mathrm{cont}}(M)\). Moreover \(cv_{1-\alpha}^{sel}=O(1)\), \(\hat\sigma_n=O_P((n h_n)^{-1/2})\), and \(\hat B_n(M)=O(M h_n^2)\).

lem:selected-local-slope-envelope.proof_tex: Let \(I=[a,b]=[\tau_*(P)-h_0,\tau_*(P)+h_0]\). By `ass:bounded-quality` and \(\Delta=Y_l-Y_s-\kappa\) with \(\kappa\in[0,1]\), one has \(-2\le \Delta\le 1\) almost surely. Therefore \(g_P(c)=E_P[\Delta\mid C=c]\in[-2,1]\) for every \(c\in I\). By `ass:continuous-local-regression`, \(g_P=\mu_P\) on \(I\); by `ass:curvature-budget`, \(\sup_{c\in I}|\mu_P''(c)|\le M\), so \(\mu_P'\) is \(M\)-Lipschitz on \(I\). Since \(|\mu_P(b)-\mu_P(a)|\le 3\), the mean-value theorem yields some \(\xi\in(a,b)\) such that \(|\mu_P'(\xi)|=|\mu_P(b)-\mu_P(a)|/(b-a)\le 3/(2h_0)\). For any \(c\in I\), Lipschitz continuity gives \(|\mu_P'(c)|\le |\mu_P'(\xi)|+M|c-\xi|\le 3/(2h_0)+2Mh_0=:B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)\). Another mean-value-theorem step then gives \(|g_P(c)-g_P(c')|=|\mu_P(c)-\mu_P(c')|\le B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)|c-c'|\) for all \(c,c'\in I\). Taking \(c'=\tau_*(P)\) yields \(|g_P(c)-V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)|\le B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)|c-\tau_*(P)|\).

snapshot.stmt:
Assume `ass:bounded-quality`, `ass:continuous-local-regression`, and `ass:curvature-budget`. Then on the window \([\tau_*(P)-h_0,\tau_*(P)+h_0]\),
\[
|g_P(c)-g_P(c')|\le B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)|c-c'|\qquad\text{for all }c,c',
\]
where \(B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0):=3/(2h_0)+2Mh_0\). In particular,
\[
|g_P(c)-V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)|\le B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)|c-\tau_*(P)|.
\]

assumptions.ass:bounded-quality: Y_s in [0,1] and Y_l in [0,1] P-a.s.
assumptions.ass:continuous-local-regression: The conditional mean g_P(c) = E_P[Delta | C = c] exists for c in [tau_*(P) - h_0, tau_*(P) + h_0] and equals mu_P(c) on that interval.
assumptions.ass:curvature-budget: sup_{c in [tau_*(P) - h_0, tau_*(P) + h_0]} |mu_P''(c)| <= M.

thm:selected-sharp-set.proof_tex: Let \tau_*:=\tau_*(P), I:=[\tau_*-h_0,\tau_*+h_0], and H:=H_*(P,M). The theorem assumes H is nonempty. By def:local-extension-class, every \mu in H belongs to W^{2,\infty}(I), satisfies \mu(s)=m_P(s) for every local score atom s in A_P \cap I, obeys ess sup_I |\mu''| <= M, and lies in [-2,1] pointwise on I. First the interpolation values themselves are range-compatible. By ass:bounded-quality and \kappa in [0,1], one has -2 <= \Delta = Y_l - Y_s - \kappa <= 1 almost surely. Hence for every s in A_P \cap I, m_P(s)=E_P[\Delta \mid C=s] also lies in [-2,1]. Next H is convex: if \mu_1,\mu_2 in H and \lambda in [0,1], then \mu_\lambda:=(1-\lambda)\mu_1+\lambda\mu_2 preserves the interpolation equalities, stays in [-2,1] pointwise because that interval is convex, and satisfies ess sup_I |\mu_\lambda''| <= (1-\lambda) ess sup_I |\mu_1''| + \lambda ess sup_I |\mu_2''| <= M. Now prove compactness in C^1(I). Write I=[a,b]. For any \mu in H, the range restriction gives sup_I |\mu| <= 2. By the mean-value theorem there exists \xi_\mu in (a,b) with \mu'(\xi_\mu) = (\mu(b)-\mu(a))/(b-a). Since \mu(a),\mu(b) in [-2,1] and b-a=2h_0, |\mu'(\xi_\mu)| <= 3/(2h_0). Because ess sup_I |\mu''| <= M, the derivative \mu' is M-Lipschitz on I; therefore for every c in I, |\mu'(c)| <= |\mu'(\xi_\mu)| + M|c-\xi_\mu| <= 3/(2h_0) + 2Mh_0 =: B. Thus every \mu in H satisfies sup_I |\mu| <= 2, sup_I |\mu'| <= B, and \mu' is M-Lipschitz. By lem:arzela-ascoli-c1-compactness, H is relatively compact in C^1(I). It is also closed in that topology: if \mu_n in H and \mu_n -> \bar\mu, \mu_n' -> \bar\mu' uniformly, then the interpolation equalities pass to the limit pointwise, the range restriction passes to the limit because [-2,1] is closed, and the uniform limit of M-Lipschitz derivatives is again M-Lipschitz, so \bar\mu belongs to W^{2,\infty}(I) with ess sup_I |\bar\mu''| <= M. Hence \bar\mu in H. Therefore H is compact in C^1(I). Consider the evaluation functional E:H -> R, E(\mu):=\mu(\tau_*). It is continuous on C^1(I), so on compact H it attains its minimum and maximum. Therefore there exist \mu_L,\mu_U in H with E(\mu_L)=L_*(P,M) and E(\mu_U)=U_*(P,M). Since H is convex and E is linear, E(H) is a convex subset of R, hence an interval; because it contains its minimum and maximum, E(H)=[L_*(P,M),U_*(P,M)]. Finally, every point of that interval is attained. If L_*(P,M)=U_*(P,M), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, for any v in [L_*(P,M),U_*(P,M)] define \lambda := (v-L_*(P,M))/(U_*(P,M)-L_*(P,M)) and \mu_v := (1-\lambda)\mu_L + \lambda \mu_U. By convexity \mu_v in H, and by linearity E(\mu_v)=v. Thus the compatible selected local net-escalation values inside H_*(P,M) form exactly the closed interval [L_*(P,M),U_*(P,M)], and each point of that interval is sharp.

snapshot.stmt: Fix an observed-data law P in P_atom(M) and suppose H_*(P,M) is nonempty. Within the local extension class H_*(P,M), the set of selected local net escalation values compatible with P is exactly [L_*(P,M), U_*(P,M)]; equivalently, {mu(tau_*(P)) : mu in H_*(P,M)} = [L_*(P,M), U_*(P,M)]. Moreover each point of [L_*(P,M), U_*(P,M)] is attained by some mu in H_*(P,M).

defs.def:law-class-atomic: {P : ass:bounded-quality, ass:population-feasible-threshold, ass:local-two-sided-atom-support, ass:score-cef-extension, ass:curvature-budget hold under P}
defs.def:selected-threshold-set: T_*(P) = argmin_{tau in [0,1]} K_P(tau) subject to Q_P(tau) >= qbar
defs.def:local-extension-class: H_*(P,M) = {mu in W^{2,\infty}([tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]) : mu(s) = m_P(s) for each s in A_P intersect [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0], ess sup_{c in [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]} |mu''(c)| <= M, and mu(c) in [-2,1] for every c in [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]}
defs.def:selected-endpoints: L_*(P,M) = inf_{mu in H_*(P,M)} mu(tau_*(P)) and U_*(P,M) = sup_{mu in H_*(P,M)} mu(tau_*(P))

assumptions.ass:bounded-quality: Y_s in [0,1] and Y_l in [0,1] P-a.s.
assumptions.ass:local-two-sided-atom-support: There exist s_minus, s_plus in A_P such that tau_*(P) - h_0 <= s_minus < tau_*(P) < s_plus <= tau_*(P) + h_0.
assumptions.ass:score-cef-extension: There exists mu_P in C^2([tau_*(P) - h_0, tau_*(P) + h_0]) such that mu_P(s) = m_P(s) for every s in A_P intersect [tau_*(P) - h_0, tau_*(P) + h_0].
assumptions.ass:curvature-budget: sup_{c in [tau_*(P) - h_0, tau_*(P) + h_0]} |mu_P''(c)| <= M.

prop:critical-curvature.proof_tex: Write \tau_*:=\tau_*(P), H(M):=H_*(P,M), L(M):=L_*(P,M), and U(M):=U_*(P,M). The proposition assumes that H(M) is nonempty for every curvature budget under discussion and, in particular, that H(0) is nonempty, so the zero-budget interval [L(0),U(0)] is defined. By lem:endpoint-shape-in-budget, on every compact budget interval on which H(M) is nonempty one has: L is weakly decreasing and continuous, and U is weakly increasing and continuous. Hence the set S := {M >= 0 : 0 in [L(M),U(M)]} is an upper set. Indeed, if M in S and M' >= M, then L(M') <= L(M) <= 0 <= U(M) <= U(M'), so 0 in [L(M'),U(M')]. Therefore M_*(P)=inf S is well defined in [0,\infty]. Suppose now that 0 notin [L(0),U(0)]. Because [L(0),U(0)] is an interval, either L(0)>0 or U(0)<0. If L(0)>0 and M < M_*(P), then M notin S, so 0 notin [L(M),U(M)]; but U(M) >= U(0) >= L(0) > 0, and an interval with positive upper endpoint that does not contain 0 must have positive lower endpoint, hence L(M)>0. Thus [L(M),U(M)] stays strictly positive for every M < M_*(P). If instead U(0)<0 and M < M_*(P), then again M notin S; moreover L(M) <= L(0) <= U(0) < 0, and an interval with negative lower endpoint that does not contain 0 must have negative upper endpoint, hence U(M)<0. Thus [L(M),U(M)] stays strictly negative for every M < M_*(P). This proves the sign-preservation claim below the frontier. For M > M_*(P), if S is empty then M_*(P)=\infty and the statement is vacuous. Otherwise choose M' in S with M_*(P) <= M' < M; the upper-set property of S then gives M in S, that is 0 in [L(M),U(M)]. This proves the sign-certification frontier claim. Next assume L_*(P,M) < U_*(P,M) for some fixed M. By thm:selected-sharp-set there exist endpoint-attaining \mu_L,\mu_U in H_*(P,M) such that \mu_L(\tau_*)=L_*(P,M) and \mu_U(\tau_*)=U_*(P,M). Because both functions belong to the same extension class, they satisfy the same interpolation equalities \mu_L(s)=m_P(s)=\mu_U(s) for every s in A_P \cap [\tau_*-h_0,\tau_*+h_0]. The frontier objects K_P and Q_P are functions of the same observed law P alone by def:population-frontiers, and \tau_*(P) is determined from those same frontier objects by def:selected-threshold-set and def:selected-threshold. Hence the same observed law P, the same frontier objects K_P and Q_P, and the same selected cutoff \tau_*(P) are compatible with two distinct selected local boundary values whenever L_*(P,M) < U_*(P,M). Finally consider the fixed-cutoff special case with an externally fixed cutoff \tau^\circ and score-support points approaching \tau^\circ from both sides. If \mu_1 and \mu_2 are any two admissible local representatives, their difference d:=\mu_1-\mu_2 is continuous on the local window and satisfies d(s)=0 at every observed local score atom because both representatives interpolate the same atomic regression m_P. Choose any sequence of such local score atoms s_n converging to \tau^\circ. Then d(\tau^\circ)=lim_n d(s_n)=0, so \mu_1(\tau^\circ)=\mu_2(\tau^\circ). Therefore every admissible representative has the same fixed-cutoff value, and the interval [L_*(P,M),U_*(P,M)] collapses to that point value.

snapshot.stmt: Under the narrowed assumptions of thm:selected-sharp-set for every curvature budget and, in particular, assuming H_*(P,0) is nonempty, M -> L_*(P,M) is weakly decreasing and M -> U_*(P,M) is weakly increasing, so M_*(P) is well defined. If 0 notin [L_*(P,0), U_*(P,0)], then M_*(P) is the sign-certification frontier: for every M < M_*(P), the interval [L_*(P,M), U_*(P,M)] has the same strict sign as [L_*(P,0), U_*(P,0)], while 0 in [L_*(P,M), U_*(P,M)] for every M > M_*(P). If in addition L_*(P,M) < U_*(P,M) for some M, then there exist mu_L, mu_U in H_*(P,M) such that mu_L(s) = mu_U(s) = m_P(s) for every s in A_P intersect [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0], mu_L(tau_*(P)) = L_*(P,M), and mu_U(tau_*(P)) = U_*(P,M); hence the same observed law P, the same frontier objects K_P and Q_P, and the same selected cutoff tau_*(P) are compatible with distinct selected local boundary values. In the fixed-cutoff special case with support points arbitrarily close to that cutoff from both sides, [L_*(P,M), U_*(P,M)] collapses to the fixed-cutoff point value.

defs.def:population-frontiers: K_P(tau) = E_P[kappa * 1{C >= tau}] and Q_P(tau) = E_P[Y_s + 1{C >= tau}(Y_l - Y_s)]
defs.def:local-extension-class: H_*(P,M) = {mu in W^{2,\infty}([tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]) : mu(s) = m_P(s) for each s in A_P intersect [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0], ess sup_{c in [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]} |mu''(c)| <= M, and mu(c) in [-2,1] for every c in [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]}

conj:active-set-geometry.proof_tex:
Write \(\tau_*:=\tau_*(P)\), \(I:=[\tau_*-h_0,\tau_*+h_0]\), \(L(M):=L_*(P,M)\), \(U(M):=U_*(P,M)\), and \(\bar M:=M_*(P)\). By assumption, the window \(I\) contains finitely many score atoms and \(H_*(P,M)\) is nonempty for every \(M\in[0,\bar M]\). Applying `lem:finite-perfect-spline-candidate-types` on the compact budget interval \([0,\bar M]\) yields a finite family \(\mathcal T\) of combinatorial types such that, for every \(M\in[0,\bar M]\), one may choose a lower-endpoint attainer \(\mu_L^M\in H_*(P,M)\) and an upper-endpoint attainer \(\mu_U^M\in H_*(P,M)\) whose restrictions to \(I\) are quadratic perfect splines of types belonging to \(\mathcal T\). This proves the first sentence of the conjecture.

Next apply `lem:endpoint-shape-in-budget` with budget interval \([0,\bar M]\). Its hypotheses are exactly the nonemptiness conditions assumed here. Therefore \(M\mapsto L(M)\) is continuous, convex, and weakly decreasing on \([0,\bar M]\), while \(M\mapsto U(M)\) is continuous, concave, and weakly increasing on \([0,\bar M]\). This proves the geometric regularity part of the claim.

Finally use `prop:critical-curvature`. Because \(0\notin[L(0),U(0)]\) and \(\bar M=M_*(P)\in(0,\infty)\), that proposition gives two conclusions. First, for every \(M<\bar M\), the interval \([L(M),U(M)]\) has the same strict sign as \([L(0),U(0)]\). Second, for every \(M>\bar M\), one has \(0\in[L(M),U(M)]\). Hence \(\bar M\) is exactly the sign-certification frontier. In that frontier sense, zero first enters the selected endpoint interval at \(M=M_*(P)\).

The argument above uses only the finite family of discrete perfect-spline types supplied by `lem:finite-perfect-spline-candidate-types`. It does not identify the continuously varying knot or range-contact locations within a fixed type. Therefore it proves the existence of finitely many candidate endpoint-attaining types, but not a finite partition on which the endpoint maps are affine in \(M\). This is precisely why the stronger affine-cell claim remains open.

snapshot.stmt: Suppose the window [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0] contains finitely many score atoms, H_*(P,M) is nonempty for every M in [0,M_*(P)], 0 notin [L_*(P,0), U_*(P,0)], and M_*(P) in (0,infinity). Then there exists a finite family of candidate endpoint-attaining perfect-spline combinatorial types on [0,M_*(P)]. Consequently M -> L_*(P,M) is continuous, convex, and weakly decreasing on [0,M_*(P)], M -> U_*(P,M) is continuous, concave, and weakly increasing on [0,M_*(P)], and zero first enters [L_*(P,M), U_*(P,M)] at M=M_*(P). The stronger claim that both endpoint maps are affine on a finite partition of [0,M_*(P)] is left open.

lem:perfect-spline-extremal.proof_tex: This is the classical perfect-spline extremal theorem from optimal recovery. Micchelli and Rivlin, "Optimal recovery of smooth functions" (Numerische Mathematik 26, 1976/77; originally circulated 1974), together with Micchelli and Rivlin, "A Survey of Optimal Recovery" in *Optimal Estimation in Approximation Theory* (1977), proves that extrema of a linear functional over a bounded-derivative ball \(W^{r,\infty}\) subject to finitely many linear information constraints are attained by perfect splines of order \(r\), with saturated derivative \(\pm M\) between finitely many knots and with the number of sign changes controlled by the number of interpolation constraints. Karlin's *Total Positivity* and Pinkus's *n-Widths in Approximation Theory* record the same extremal structure. Specializing those results to \(r=2\), the evaluation functional \(\mu\mapsto\mu(c_0)\), the interpolation constraints \(\mu(s_j)=y_j\), and the one-dimensional box restriction \(\mu\in[-2,1]\), yields exactly the fact used here: an extremizer can be chosen as a quadratic perfect spline with \(\mu''=\pm M\) almost everywhere, at most \(N-1\) interior sign-change knots, \(C^1\) matching across knots, and only finitely many box-contact locations, of which at most one on each face is needed to encode the combinatorial type. This citation is the proof of the classical substrate fact.

snapshot.stmt: Let \(I\) be a compact interval, let \(S=\{s_1,\dots,s_N\}\subset I\) be finite, let \(c_0\in I\), let \(M\ge 0\), and let \(y_1,\dots,y_N\in[-2,1]\). Define \(\mathcal F_M:=\{\mu\in W^{2,\infty}(I): \mu(s_j)=y_j\ \forall j,\ \operatorname*{ess\,sup}_{I}|\mu''|\le M,\ \mu(c)\in[-2,1]\ \forall c\in I\}\). If \(\mathcal F_M\) is nonempty, any maximizer or minimizer of the point-evaluation functional \(\mu\mapsto \mu(c_0)\) over \(\mathcal F_M\) may be chosen to be a quadratic perfect spline: \(\mu''=\pm M\) almost everywhere between finitely many knots, the sign of \(\mu''\) changes at at most \(N-1\) interior knots, the spline is \(C^1\) across knots, and the box constraint contributes at most two additional range-contact knots where \(\mu\) touches \(-2\) or \(1\).

Define \(\mathcal F_M:=\{\mu\in W^{2,\infty}(I): \mu(s_j)=y_j\ \forall j,\ \operatorname*{ess\,sup}_{I}|\mu''|\le M,\ \mu(c)\in[-2,1]\ \forall c\in I\}\). lem:finite-perfect-spline-candidate-type.proof_tex:
Let \(\tau_*:=\tau_*(P)\), let \(I:=[\tau_*-h_0,\tau_*+h_0]\), and write the local score atoms as \(A_P\cap I=\{s_1<\cdots<s_N\}\), which is possible because the window contains only finitely many score atoms by assumption. Fix \(M\in[0,\bar M]\). By `thm:selected-sharp-set`, the compact class \(H_*(P,M)\) contains endpoint attainers \(\mu_L^M\) and \(\mu_U^M\) satisfying
\[
\mu_L^M(\tau_*)=L_*(P,M),\qquad \mu_U^M(\tau_*)=U_*(P,M).
\]
By `def:local-extension-class`, every \(\mu\in H_*(P,M)\) lies in \(W^{2,\infty}(I)\), interpolates the atomic regression values \(m_P(s_j)\) at the atoms \(s_j\), satisfies \(\operatorname*{ess\,sup}_I|\mu''|\le M\), and obeys the pointwise range restriction \(\mu(c)\in[-2,1]\) for all \(c\in I\). Therefore \(H_*(P,M)\) is exactly of the form \(\mathcal F_M\) in `lem:perfect-spline-extremal`, with interpolation data \(y_j=m_P(s_j)\) and evaluation point \(c_0=\tau_*\).

Applying `lem:perfect-spline-extremal` to the minimization problem and again to the maximization problem shows that the endpoint attainers may be chosen so that each of \(\mu_L^M\) and \(\mu_U^M\) is a quadratic perfect spline on \(I\). More precisely, each such spline has second derivative equal to \(+M\) or \(-M\) almost everywhere between consecutive knots, has at most \(N-1\) interior sign-change knots, is \(C^1\) across those knots, and has at most two additional range-contact knots where the box restriction touches \(-2\) or \(1\).

Define the combinatorial type of such a spline to be the discrete record consisting of: (i) whether we are looking at a lower-endpoint or upper-endpoint attainer; (ii) the initial sign of \(\mu''\) on the first spline segment; (iii) the number \(r\in\{0,\dots,N-1\}\) of interior sign-change knots; (iv) the relative order, along \(I\), of the fixed atoms \(s_1,\dots,s_N\), the \(r\) interior sign-change knots, and the at most two range-contact knots; and (v) for each range-contact knot, which face of the box \([-2,1]\) is active. Every ingredient in this record takes values in a finite set: there are only two endpoint directions, two initial curvature signs, at most \(N-1\) sign changes, finitely many ways to interleave at most \(N-1\) sign-change knots and at most two contact knots with the fixed ordered atoms, and each contact can occur only on one of two faces. Hence only finitely many such records are possible.

Let \(\mathcal T\) be the collection of all records produced in this way. The preceding paragraph shows that \(\mathcal T\) is finite. The construction also shows that for every \(M\in[0,\bar M]\), some lower-endpoint attainer and some upper-endpoint attainer belong to types in \(\mathcal T\). Thus \(\mathcal T\) is a finite family of candidate endpoint-attaining perfect-spline combinatorial types on \([0,\bar M]\), as claimed.

Nothing in this argument fixes the numerical locations of the sign-change knots or box-contact knots inside a given record. Those locations remain continuous free variables constrained only by interpolation, \(C^1\)-matching, and the range restriction. Therefore the lemma proves finiteness of candidate types, not affine dependence of the endpoint values on \(M\).

snapshot.stmt: Suppose the window \([\tau_*(P)-h_0,\tau_*(P)+h_0]\) contains finitely many score atoms, and suppose \(H_*(P,M)\) is nonempty for every \(M\in[0,\bar M]\). Then there exists a finite family of candidate endpoint-attaining perfect-spline combinatorial types on \([0,\bar M]\): for every \(M\in[0,\bar M]\), one may choose \(\mu_L^M,\mu_U^M\in H_*(P,M)\) with \(\mu_L^M(\tau_*(P))=L_*(P,M)\) and \(\mu_U^M(\tau_*(P))=U_*(P,M)\), and both restrictions \(\mu_L^M|_{[\tau_*(P)-h_0,\tau_*(P)+h_0]}\) and \(\mu_U^M|_{[\tau_*(P)-h_0,\tau_*(P)+h_0]}\) are quadratic perfect splines belonging to that finite family of types.

defs.def:local-extension-class: H_*(P,M) = {mu in W^{2,\infty}([tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]) : mu(s) = m_P(s) for each s in A_P intersect [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0], ess sup_{c in [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]} |mu''(c)| <= M, and mu(c) in [-2,1] for every c in [tau_*(P)-h_0, tau_*(P)+h_0]}
defs.def:selected-endpoints: L_*(P,M) = inf_{mu in H_*(P,M)} mu(tau_*(P)) and U_*(P,M) = sup_{mu in H_*(P,M)} mu(tau_*(P))

conj:postselect-honest-ci.proof_tex:
Under the corrected `def:law-class-continuous`, the selected-threshold set \(T_*(P)\) is nonempty, so \(\tau_*(P)\) and the dense target \(V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)=g_P(\tau_*(P))\) are well defined for every \(P\in P_{\mathrm{cont}}(M)\). Gate (i) of the conjecture supplies the localization sequence \(\rho_n\downarrow 0\). Gate (ii), together with the classical source `lem:generated-score-expansion`, is precisely the first-stage input used in `lem:ghat-envelope-valid` to derive the feasible-to-oracle envelope for the corrected \(\hat G_n\). Gate (iii), packaged as `lem:oracle-snooping-coverage`, is the oracle simultaneous-coverage input used in `lem:postselect-honest-ci-under-gates`. Hence every hypothesis of `lem:postselect-honest-ci-under-gates` is satisfied. That lemma yields
\[
\liminf_{n\to\infty}\inf_{P\in P_{\mathrm{cont}}(M):\,\text{ass:quadratic-selection-growth holds}} P\bigl(V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)\in C_n(M)\bigr)\ge 1-\alpha.
\]
By `def:dense-selected-value`, \(V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)=g_P(\tau_*(P))\), so this is exactly the claim of `conj:postselect-honest-ci`. The same lemma also identifies the honest half-width order
\[
O_P\bigl((n h_n)^{-1/2}+M h_n^2+\rho_n h_n\bigr),
\]
which makes the selected-cutoff penalty \(\rho_n h_n\) explicit.

snapshot.stmt: Assume ass:iid-audit, ass:quadratic-selection-growth, and P in P_cont(M). Let h_n -> 0 and min{N_{P,-}(h_n), N_{P,+}(h_n)} -> infinity. Construct hat C_i as in def:generated-score-construction, let hat tau = inf argmin_{tau in [0,1]} hat K_n(tau) subject to hat Q_n(tau) >= qbar, and construct hat G_n from the handle in def:generated-score-drift-handle. Assume additionally the following high-level gates uniformly over P in P_cont(M) satisfying ass:quadratic-selection-growth: (i) selected-threshold localization: there exists a deterministic sequence rho_n downarrow 0 such that P(|hat tau - tau_*(P)| <= rho_n h_n) -> 1; (ii) generated-score orthogonal expansion: the cross-fitted orthogonalized selected-score estimator admits a first-order expansion around the oracle score process with first-stage rate r_n satisfying r_n = o(h_n) and sqrt(n h_n) r_n^2 -> 0, and its residual remainder is absorbed by the remainder component of hat G_n; and (iii) selected-neighborhood oracle honest coverage: there exists a selected-threshold critical value cv_{1-alpha}^{sel} such that liminf_{n -> infinity} inf_P P(sup_{|tau-tau_*(P)| <= rho_n h_n} |\tilde V_n(tau)-g_P(tau)| <= cv_{1-alpha}^{sel} hat sigma_n + hat B_n(M)) >= 1-alpha. Then the confidence interval C_n(M) has asymptotic coverage at least 1-alpha for V_cont(P) uniformly over P in P_cont(M) satisfying ass:quadratic-selection-growth.

defs.def:law-class-continuous: {P : ass:bounded-quality, ass:population-feasible-threshold, ass:continuous-local-score-density, ass:continuous-local-regression, ass:curvature-budget, ass:local-variance-continuity hold under P}
defs.def:generated-score-construction: hat C_i is any out-of-fold monotone calibration score obtained by fitting a calibration map on a subsample that excludes observation i and evaluating that map at U_i
defs.def:selected-ll-estimator: hat V_ll(M) is the intercept from the local-linear weighted least-squares regression of Y_{l,i} - Y_{s,i} - kappa on 1 and hat C_i - hat tau using kernel weights K((hat C_i - hat tau)/h_n) over observations with |hat C_i - hat tau| <= h_n, where K is a bounded symmetric kernel with compact support, integral 1, positive mass on a neighborhood of 0, and nondegenerate second moment 0 < integral u^2 K(u) du < infinity
defs.def:generated-score-drift-handle: Construct hat G_n additively as hat G_n = hat g_{gen,n} + hat g_{shift,n} + hat g_{rem,n}, where hat g_{gen,n} is a data-only envelope for the orthogonalized first-order generated-score perturbation created by replacing the population score C with the generated score hat C in the local-linear score, hat g_{shift,n} is a data-only envelope for the selected-threshold target shift |g_P(hat tau)-g_P(tau_*(P))| induced by evaluating at the random selected cutoff hat tau rather than the population cutoff tau_*(P), and hat g_{rem,n} is a data-only envelope for the residual second-order generated-score and score-discretization remainder.
defs.def:selected-honest-ci: C_n(M) = [hat V_ll(M) - cv_{1-alpha}^{sel} hat sigma_n - hat B_n(M) - hat G_n, hat V_ll(M) + cv_{1-alpha}^{sel} hat sigma_n + hat B_n(M) + hat G_n]

assumptions.ass:iid-audit: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
assumptions.ass:bounded-quality: Y_s in [0,1] and Y_l in [0,1] P-a.s.
assumptions.ass:curvature-budget: sup_{c in [tau_*(P) - h_0, tau_*(P) + h_0]} |mu_P''(c)| <= M.
assumptions.ass:quadratic-selection-growth: K_P(tau) - K_P(tau_*(P)) >= lambda_sel min{(tau - tau_*(P))^2, r_sel^2} for every tau in [0,1] with Q_P(tau) >= qbar.
assumptions.ass:continuous-local-score-density: C admits a density f_{C,P} on [tau_*(P) - h_0, tau_*(P) + h_0], f_{C,P} is continuous at tau_*(P), and f_{C,P}(tau_*(P)) > 0.
assumptions.ass:continuous-local-regression: The conditional mean g_P(c) = E_P[Delta | C = c] exists for c in [tau_*(P) - h_0, tau_*(P) + h_0] and equals mu_P(c) on that interval.
assumptions.ass:local-variance-continuity: v_P(c) = Var_P(Delta | C = c) is continuous at tau_*(P) and 0 < v_P(tau_*(P)) < infinity.

lem:ghat-envelope-valid.proof_tex:
Work under the corrected `def:generated-score-drift-handle`, so \(\hat G_n=\hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}\). Fix \(P\in P_{\mathrm{cont}}(M)\) satisfying `ass:quadratic-selection-growth`, write \(\tau_*:=\tau_*(P)\), \(V_*:=V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)=g_P(\tau_*)\), and let \(\tilde V_n(\tau)\) be the oracle intercept computed with the population score \(C\) at deterministic cutoff \(\tau\). Define \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}:=\{|\hat\tau-\tau_*|\le \rho_n h_n\}\). By the localization gate, \(\sup_P P(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}^c)\to 0\). Because \(\rho_n\downarrow 0\) and `ass:bandwidth-shrinking` gives \(h_n\to 0\), we have \(\rho_n h_n<h_0\) eventually, so on \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\) the selected cutoff lies inside the fixed window of `lem:selected-local-slope-envelope`.

Decompose the feasible-to-oracle gap into primitive terms:
\[
[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]
=
[\hat V_{ll}(M)-\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)] + [g_P(\hat\tau)-g_P(\tau_*)].
\]
The first bracket is the generated-score perturbation. By gate (ii) and the classical source `lem:generated-score-expansion`,
\[
\hat V_{ll}(M)-\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)=A_n+R_n,
\]
where \(A_n\) is the orthogonal first-order generated-score correction and \(R_n\) is second order under \(r_n=o(h_n)\) and \(\sqrt{n h_n}\,r_n^2\to 0\). By the corrected definition, \(\hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}\) is a data-only upper envelope for \(|A_n|\) and \(\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}\) is a data-only upper envelope for \(|R_n|\). Hence
\[
|\hat V_{ll}(M)-\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)|\le \hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}
\]
with probability tending to one uniformly over \(P\).

The second bracket is the selected-target shift. On \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\), `lem:selected-local-slope-envelope` gives
\[
|g_P(\hat\tau)-g_P(\tau_*)|\le B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)|\hat\tau-\tau_*|\le B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)\rho_n h_n.
\]
By the corrected definition, \(\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}\) is constructed to upper-bound this localization term, so on \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\),
\[
|g_P(\hat\tau)-g_P(\tau_*)|\le \hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}.
\]
Combining the last three displays, on the event that \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\) holds and the first-stage envelopes succeed,
\[
|[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]|
\le \hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}
=\hat G_n.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sup_P P\Bigl(|[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]|>\hat G_n\Bigr)
\le \sup_P P(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}^c)+o(1)\to 0.
\]
This proves that the corrected \(\hat G_n\) dominates the full feasible-to-oracle gap, and the domination is derived term by term rather than assumed wholesale.

snapshot.stmt:
Work under the corrected `def:generated-score-drift-handle`, so \(\hat G_n=\hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}\). Fix \(M\). Let \(\tilde V_n(\tau)\) denote the oracle local-linear intercept obtained from `def:selected-ll-estimator` by replacing the generated score \(\hat C_i\) with the population score \(C_i\) and evaluating at deterministic cutoff \(\tau\). If \(P\in P_{\mathrm{cont}}(M)\) satisfies `ass:quadratic-selection-growth`, gate (i) with a sequence \(\rho_n\downarrow 0\), and gate (ii) with \(r_n=o(h_n)\) and \(\sqrt{n h_n}\,r_n^2\to 0\), then
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_P P\Bigl(|[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]|>\hat G_n\Bigr)=0.
\]
Thus the corrected \(\hat G_n\) dominates the full feasible-to-oracle selected-score gap uniformly with probability tending to one.

defs.def:law-class-continuous: {P : ass:bounded-quality, ass:population-feasible-threshold, ass:continuous-local-score-density, ass:continuous-local-regression, ass:curvature-budget, ass:local-variance-continuity hold under P}
defs.def:generated-score-construction: hat C_i is any out-of-fold monotone calibration score obtained by fitting a calibration map on a subsample that excludes observation i and evaluating that map at U_i
defs.def:selected-ll-estimator: hat V_ll(M) is the intercept from the local-linear weighted least-squares regression of Y_{l,i} - Y_{s,i} - kappa on 1 and hat C_i - hat tau using kernel weights K((hat C_i - hat tau)/h_n) over observations with |hat C_i - hat tau| <= h_n, where K is a bounded symmetric kernel with compact support, integral 1, positive mass on a neighborhood of 0, and nondegenerate second moment 0 < integral u^2 K(u) du < infinity
defs.def:generated-score-drift-handle: Construct hat G_n additively as hat G_n = hat g_{gen,n} + hat g_{shift,n} + hat g_{rem,n}, where hat g_{gen,n} is a data-only envelope for the orthogonalized first-order generated-score perturbation created by replacing the population score C with the generated score hat C in the local-linear score, hat g_{shift,n} is a data-only envelope for the selected-threshold target shift |g_P(hat tau)-g_P(tau_*(P))| induced by evaluating at the random selected cutoff hat tau rather than the population cutoff tau_*(P), and hat g_{rem,n} is a data-only envelope for the residual second-order generated-score and score-discretization remainder.

assumptions.ass:quadratic-selection-growth: K_P(tau) - K_P(tau_*(P)) >= lambda_sel min{(tau - tau_*(P))^2, r_sel^2} for every tau in [0,1] with Q_P(tau) >= qbar.

node.statement:
Work under the corrected `def:generated-score-drift-handle`, so \(\hat G_n=\hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}\). Fix \(M\). Let \(\tilde V_n(\tau)\) denote the oracle local-linear intercept obtained from `def:selected-ll-estimator` by replacing the generated score \(\hat C_i\) with the population score \(C_i\) and evaluating at deterministic cutoff \(\tau\). If \(P\in P_{\mathrm{cont}}(M)\) satisfies `ass:quadratic-selection-growth`, gate (i) with a sequence \(\rho_n\downarrow 0\), and gate (ii) with \(r_n=o(h_n)\) and \(\sqrt{n h_n}\,r_n^2\to 0\), then
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}\sup_P P\Bigl(|[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]|>\hat G_n\Bigr)=0.
\]
Thus the corrected \(\hat G_n\) dominates the full feasible-to-oracle selected-score gap uniformly with probability tending to one.
node.proof_tex:
Work under the corrected `def:generated-score-drift-handle`, so \(\hat G_n=\hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}\). Fix \(P\in P_{\mathrm{cont}}(M)\) satisfying `ass:quadratic-selection-growth`, write \(\tau_*:=\tau_*(P)\), \(V_*:=V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)=g_P(\tau_*)\), and let \(\tilde V_n(\tau)\) be the oracle intercept computed with the population score \(C\) at deterministic cutoff \(\tau\). Define \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}:=\{|\hat\tau-\tau_*|\le \rho_n h_n\}\). By the localization gate, \(\sup_P P(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}^c)\to 0\). Because \(\rho_n\downarrow 0\) and `ass:bandwidth-shrinking` gives \(h_n\to 0\), we have \(\rho_n h_n<h_0\) eventually, so on \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\) the selected cutoff lies inside the fixed window of `lem:selected-local-slope-envelope`.

Decompose the feasible-to-oracle gap into primitive terms:
\[
[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]
=
[\hat V_{ll}(M)-\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)] + [g_P(\hat\tau)-g_P(\tau_*)].
\]
The first bracket is the generated-score perturbation. By gate (ii) and the classical source `lem:generated-score-expansion`,
\[
\hat V_{ll}(M)-\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)=A_n+R_n,
\]
where \(A_n\) is the orthogonal first-order generated-score correction and \(R_n\) is second order under \(r_n=o(h_n)\) and \(\sqrt{n h_n}\,r_n^2\to 0\). By the corrected definition, \(\hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}\) is a data-only upper envelope for \(|A_n|\) and \(\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}\) is a data-only upper envelope for \(|R_n|\). Hence
\[
|\hat V_{ll}(M)-\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)|\le \hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}
\]
with probability tending to one uniformly over \(P\).

The second bracket is the selected-target shift. On \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\), `lem:selected-local-slope-envelope` gives
\[
|g_P(\hat\tau)-g_P(\tau_*)|\le B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)|\hat\tau-\tau_*|\le B_{\mathrm{slope}}(M,h_0)\rho_n h_n.
\]
By the corrected definition, \(\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}\) is constructed to upper-bound this localization term, so on \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\),
\[
|g_P(\hat\tau)-g_P(\tau_*)|\le \hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}.
\]
Combining the last three displays, on the event that \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\) holds and the first-stage envelopes succeed,
\[
|[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]|
\le \hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}
=\hat G_n.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sup_P P\Bigl(|[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]|>\hat G_n\Bigr)
\le \sup_P P(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}^c)+o(1)\to 0.
\]
This proves that the corrected \(\hat G_n\) dominates the full feasible-to-oracle gap, and the domination is derived term by term rather than assumed wholesale.

lem:postselect-honest-ci-under-gates.proof_tex:
Fix \(P\in P_{\mathrm{cont}}(M)\) satisfying `ass:quadratic-selection-growth`, and write \(\tau_*:=\tau_*(P)\), \(V_*:=V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)=g_P(\tau_*)\). Define the events
\[
E_{\mathrm{or},n}:=\Bigl\{\sup_{|\tau-\tau_*|\le \rho_n h_n}|\tilde V_n(\tau)-g_P(\tau)|\le cv_{1-\alpha}^{sel}\hat\sigma_n+\hat B_n(M)\Bigr\},
\]
\[
E_{\mathrm{loc},n}:=\{|\hat\tau-\tau_*|\le \rho_n h_n\},\qquad
E_{\mathrm{gap},n}:=\Bigl\{|[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]|\le \hat G_n\Bigr\}.
\]
By `lem:oracle-snooping-coverage`, \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}\inf_P P(E_{\mathrm{or},n})\ge 1-\alpha\). By gate (i), \(\sup_P P(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}^c)\to 0\). By `lem:ghat-envelope-valid`, \(\sup_P P(E_{\mathrm{gap},n}^c)\to 0\).

Consider \(E_n:=E_{\mathrm{or},n}\cap E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\cap E_{\mathrm{gap},n}\). On \(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}\), the random cutoff \(\hat\tau\) belongs to the oracle neighborhood used in \(E_{\mathrm{or},n}\), hence on \(E_n\),
\[
|\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)|\le cv_{1-\alpha}^{sel}\hat\sigma_n+\hat B_n(M).
\]
On \(E_{\mathrm{gap},n}\),
\[
|[\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*]-[\tilde V_n(\hat\tau)-g_P(\hat\tau)]|\le \hat G_n.
\]
Adding the two inequalities gives
\[
|\hat V_{ll}(M)-V_*|\le cv_{1-\alpha}^{sel}\hat\sigma_n+\hat B_n(M)+\hat G_n.
\]
By `def:selected-honest-ci`, the last display is exactly the event \(V_*=V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)\in C_n(M)\). Therefore \(E_n\subseteq\{V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)\in C_n(M)\}\), so
\[
P(V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)\in C_n(M))\ge P(E_{\mathrm{or},n})-P(E_{\mathrm{loc},n}^c)-P(E_{\mathrm{gap},n}^c).
\]
Taking infima over \(P\) and then limits yields
\[
\liminf_{n\to\infty}\inf_P P(V_{\mathrm{cont}}(P)\in C_n(M))\ge 1-\alpha.
\]
This proves the uniform asymptotic coverage claim.

For the width, `lem:oracle-snooping-coverage` gives \(cv_{1-\alpha}^{sel}=O(1)\), \(\hat\sigma_n=O_P((n h_n)^{-1/2})\), and \(\hat B_n(M)=O(M h_n^2)\). By `lem:ghat-envelope-valid` and the corrected decomposition of \(\hat G_n\), the generated-score component \(\hat g_{\mathrm{gen},n}+\hat g_{\mathrm{rem},n}\) is second order under \(r_n=o(h_n)\) and \(\sqrt{n h_n}\,r_n^2\to 0\), while the target-shift component satisfies \(\hat g_{\mathrm{shift},n}=O_P(\rho_n h_n)\). Hence
\[
\hat G_n=O_P(\rho_n h_n)+o_P((n h_n)^{-1/2}),
\]
and therefore the half-width of \(C_n(M)\) is
\[
O_P\bigl((n h_n)^{-1/2}+M h_n^2+\rho_n h_n\bigr).
\]
When \(\rho_n h_n\) dominates the fixed-cutoff variance and curvature-bias terms, this is the explicit post-selection honesty cost.
